methodology_sections: 1. Lifecycle Integrity - State transitions, illegal states, predecessors
2. Monotonicity & Irreversibility - Increasing values, one-way transitions
3. Access & Permission Logic - Conditional access, role requirements
4. Time & Resource Integrity - Time-locks, resource bounds, expiry

invariants.description: Setting yieldFeePercentage to FEE_PRECISION (1e9, i.e. 100%) is allowed by _setYieldFeePercentage (uses > instead of >=), but causes a division by zero in liquidate when computing the yield fee increase: (FEE_PRECISION - _yieldFeePercentage) becomes 0.
invariants.attack_scenario: Owner (malicious or mistaken) sets yieldFeePercentage to 1e9. Any subsequent call to liquidate reverts with division by zero, permanently bricking the liquidation mechanism and preventing yield from being converted to prizes. The vault becomes useless for prize generation.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_yieldFeePercentageLtPrecision() public view { assert(_yieldFeePercentage < FEE_PRECISION); }

invariants.description: The prize pool token balance must always be at least the accounted balance (sum of all available contributions plus disbursed minus withdrawn). If violated, prize claims or reward withdrawals will fail or steal from other users' allocations.
invariants.attack_scenario: If contributions are over-counted or withdrawals are under-counted, the accounted balance exceeds the actual token balance. Subsequent claims would transfer tokens that belong to other prize tiers or the reserve, leading to insolvency.

invariants.description: lastClosedDrawId must strictly increase by exactly 1 on each successful closeDraw call. Skipping or repeating draw IDs would corrupt tier liquidity calculations, contribution tracking, and claim records.
invariants.attack_scenario: If lastClosedDrawId could skip (e.g., jump from 5 to 7), contributions for draw 6 would be lost. If it could repeat, prizes for the same draw could be double-distributed. The draw accumulator's ring buffer assumes monotonic draw IDs.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_drawIdIncrement(uint16 oldDrawId) public view { assert(lastClosedDrawId == oldDrawId + 1); }

invariants.description: The global prizeTokenPerShare must be monotonically non-decreasing across draw transitions. A decrease would mean liquidity was un-distributed, causing tier remaining liquidity calculations to underflow or return incorrect values.
invariants.attack_scenario: If prizeTokenPerShare decreased, _getTierRemainingLiquidity could underflow (since it computes prizeTokenPerShare - tierPrizeTokenPerShare). Tiers would appear to have negative liquidity, and _computePrizeSize could return inflated values allowing over-claiming.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_prizeTokenPerShareNonDecreasing(uint256 oldValue) public view { assert(UD34x4.unwrap(prizeTokenPerShare) >= oldValue); }

invariants.description: A prize identified by (vault, winner, drawId, tier, prizeIndex) can only be claimed once. The claimedPrizes mapping must transition from false to true exactly once and never revert back.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the double-claim check were bypassed, a winner (or claimer acting on their behalf) could drain the prize tier's liquidity by repeatedly claiming the same prize, potentially exhausting both tier liquidity and reserves.
invariants.invariant_logic: For any (vault, winner, drawId, tier, prizeIndex): once claimedPrizes[v][w][d][t][p] == true, it cannot become false (for the same drawId)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_noDoubleClaim(address v, address w, uint16 d, uint8 t, uint32 p) public view { if (claimedPrizes[v][w][d][t][p]) { assert(claimedPrizes[v][w][d][t][p]); } }

invariants.description: setDrawManager can be called by anyone when drawManager == address(0). Since the constructor allows initializing drawManager to address(0), the first caller to setDrawManager gains permanent control over closeDraw and withdrawReserve. This is explicitly front-runnable.
invariants.attack_scenario: The deployer constructs PrizePool with drawManager=address(0), intending to set it in a follow-up transaction. An attacker front-runs and calls setDrawManager(attacker). The attacker now controls draw closures (can set arbitrary winning random numbers) and can withdraw the entire reserve.
invariants.invariant_logic: setDrawManager should only be callable by a trusted party, OR drawManager should always be set in the constructor
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_drawManagerSet() public view { assert(drawManager != address(0)); }

invariants.description: For every tier i, _tiers[i].prizeTokenPerShare must never exceed the global prizeTokenPerShare. The difference represents remaining liquidity; exceeding it would mean the tier consumed more liquidity than allocated.
invariants.attack_scenario: If a tier's prizeTokenPerShare exceeded global, _getTierRemainingLiquidity would return 0 (due to the gte check), but the tier would effectively have negative liquidity. Subsequent claims would always dip into reserves even when there should be tier liquidity, draining reserves faster than intended.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_tierPTPS(uint8 tier) public view { assert(UD34x4.unwrap(_tiers[tier].prizeTokenPerShare) <= UD34x4.unwrap(prizeTokenPerShare)); }

invariants.description: closeDraw must only be callable after the current draw period has ended (block.timestamp >= _openDrawEndsAt()). Closing early would allow the draw manager to manipulate which contributions are included and potentially game the random number against a known contribution set.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the time check were bypassed, a malicious draw manager could close the draw immediately after contributing, before other vaults can contribute. This would give their vault an outsized vault portion, increasing their users' winning probability.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_drawTiming() public view { assert(block.timestamp >= _openDrawEndsAt() || lastClosedDrawId == lastClosedDrawId); }

invariants.description: setLiquidationPair grants the new LP unlimited approval to transfer the underlying asset (e.g., USDC) from the vault. This is unnecessary since the liquidation flow only involves minting vault shares and contributing prize tokens—the LP should never need to pull underlying assets.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the LP contract has a vulnerability, or the owner maliciously sets a rogue LP, the LP can call asset.transferFrom(vault, attacker, vault_asset_balance) to drain any underlying assets held by the vault (including assets in transit during deposit). This bypasses the intended yield-only liquidation flow.
invariants.invariant_logic: The liquidation pair should not have approval to spend the vault's underlying asset. Only the yield vault should have such approval.

invariants.description: The _deposit function skips transferring tokens from the caller when the vault already holds sufficient underlying assets (_assets <= vault balance). This allows a depositor to receive shares without paying if the vault has a non-zero underlying balance.
invariants.attack_scenario: If underlying tokens end up in the vault (e.g., via direct transfer, yield vault refund, or during liquidation when _amountOut < _vaultAssets), the next depositor can call deposit() with _assets <= vault balance. They receive shares backed by the vault's existing assets, effectively stealing value from existing shareholders through dilution.
invariants.invariant_logic: In _deposit, the total amount transferred from the caller must equal _assets (the full deposit amount), regardless of the vault's existing balance
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_depositPullsTokens(uint256 callerBalanceBefore, uint256 callerBalanceAfter, uint256 _assets) public pure { assert(callerBalanceBefore - callerBalanceAfter >= _assets); }

invariants.description: After closeDraw, _winningRandomNumber must be non-zero. A zero random number would cause TierCalculationLib.calculatePseudoRandomNumber to produce deterministic hashes that could be predicted, and the modulo operation in isWinner would always yield 0, making every user a winner for tiers where winningZone > 0.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the zero-check in closeDraw were bypassed, the pseudo-random number for all users would be keccak256(abi.encode(user, tier, prizeIndex, 0)). While not technically zero, the winning random number itself being zero undermines the randomness assumption. The explicit check prevents any edge case in RNG providers.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_randomNumberNonZero() public view { if (lastClosedDrawId > 0) { assert(_winningRandomNumber != 0); } }

invariants.description: numberOfTiers must always be within [MINIMUM_NUMBER_OF_TIERS, MAXIMUM_NUMBER_OF_TIERS] (i.e., [3, 15]). Values outside this range would cause _tierOdds to return sd(0), _canaryPrizeCountFractional to return ud(0), and _estimatedPrizeCount to return 0, breaking prize distribution entirely.
invariants.attack_scenario: If numberOfTiers fell below 3, tier odds lookups would return 0, making all winning zone calculations return 0 (nobody wins). If above 15, the same would happen. The tier expansion/contraction logic in _computeNextNumberOfTiers must respect these bounds.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_tierBounds() public view { assert(numberOfTiers >= MINIMUM_NUMBER_OF_TIERS && numberOfTiers <= MAXIMUM_NUMBER_OF_TIERS); }

invariants.description: The beforeClaimPrize hook allows a winner to redirect prize tokens to an arbitrary address. The afterClaimPrize hook can revert, preventing the claim transaction from succeeding. A malicious hook implementation can grief the claimer by reverting on afterClaimPrize after the prize has been transferred.
invariants.attack_scenario: A winner sets a hook where afterClaimPrize always reverts. Any claimer attempting to claim that winner's prize will have their transaction revert. The winner cannot be claimed for, and their tier's prize liquidity remains locked, potentially affecting claim statistics and tier expansion logic.
invariants.invariant_logic: Prize claim for a winner must succeed regardless of hook behavior, OR hook failures must be caught and not block the claim
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_claimNotBlockedByHook() public { /* hook call should be wrapped in try/catch */ }

invariants.description: Deposits and liquidations must only proceed when the vault is collateralized (exchange rate >= 1 asset unit). However, there is no collateralization check on the withdraw/redeem path. If the vault becomes undercollateralized, depositors cannot enter but existing users can exit, potentially at a loss. The lack of a pause mechanism means the vault cannot be stabilized.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the yield vault suffers a loss, the exchange rate drops below 1 asset unit. New deposits are blocked (maxDeposit returns 0), but withdrawals proceed at the deflated rate. Users racing to withdraw first get more value than later users. The yieldFeeTotalSupply acts as a buffer but may be insufficient.
invariants.invariant_logic: When !_isVaultCollateralized(): maxDeposit == 0 AND maxMint == 0 AND liquidate reverts AND mintYieldFee reverts
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_undercollateralizedBlocks() public view { if (!_isVaultCollateralized()) { assert(maxDeposit(address(0)) == 0 && maxMint(address(0)) == 0); } }

invariants.description: The draw ID in the accumulator's ring buffer must be monotonically non-decreasing. The add function enforces _drawId >= newestDrawId. If violated, the binary search in getDisbursedBetween would return incorrect results, corrupting contribution calculations and vault portions.
invariants.attack_scenario: If a contribution were added for an older draw ID, the ring buffer ordering assumption would be violated. Binary search would produce incorrect observation pairs, leading to wrong disbursement amounts. Vaults could receive inflated or deflated portions of the prize pool.
invariants.invariant_logic: For any two consecutive entries i, j in drawRingBuffer where j follows i: drawRingBuffer[j] >= drawRingBuffer[i]
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_accumulatorDrawIdMonotonic(DrawAccumulatorLib.Accumulator storage acc) internal view { /* verify ring buffer ordering */ }

invariants.description: _getVaultUserBalanceAndTotalSupplyTwab truncates timestamps from uint64 to uint32 when calling TwabController. If _lastClosedDrawStartedAt + drawPeriodSeconds exceeds type(uint32).max (~year 2106), the truncation produces incorrect TWAB lookup timestamps, leading to wrong balance calculations.
invariants.attack_scenario: After year 2106, or if the protocol is deployed with a very large firstDrawStartsAt, the uint32 cast truncates the timestamp. The TWAB lookup uses a wrong time range, potentially returning zero balances for all users (nobody wins) or incorrect balances (wrong winners).
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_timestampFitsUint32() public view { assert(uint256(_lastClosedDrawStartedAt) + uint256(drawPeriodSeconds) <= type(uint32).max); }

invariants.description: contributePrizeTokens has no access control and validates contribution amount against the delta balance (unaccounted tokens in the pool). If multiple callers contribute in the same block, the first caller's contribution reduces the delta balance available for subsequent callers. A frontrunner can attribute another vault's transferred tokens to their own vault.
invariants.attack_scenario: Vault A transfers 1000 prize tokens to the prize pool and calls contributePrizeTokens(vaultA, 1000). An attacker frontruns with contributePrizeTokens(attackerVault, 1000). The delta balance check passes for the attacker (tokens are present), and vault A's subsequent call reverts because the delta is now 0. The attacker's vault gets credit for vault A's contribution.
invariants.invariant_logic: The vault portion of contributions should only credit the actual sender of tokens, not allow arbitrary vault attribution
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_contributionMatchesSender() public view { /* each contribution amount should correspond to tokens actually sent by that vault's flow */ }

invariants.description: In _consumeLiquidity, the UD34x4 precision (4 decimal places) causes rounding when computing remainingLiquidity and the prizeTokenPerShare delta. Over many claims, this rounding error accumulates, causing the reserve to be consumed slightly more than necessary or tier liquidity to be slightly misaccounted.
invariants.attack_scenario: Each prize claim loses up to 1e14 wei of precision in the tier's prizeTokenPerShare update. Over thousands of claims across many draws, this accumulated rounding error causes the effective token balance to drift from the expected balance. Eventually, the reserve could be depleted prematurely, causing legitimate prize claims to revert with InsufficientLiquidity.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_precisionLoss() public view { /* verify sum of tier remaining liquidity + reserve <= balance - totalWithdrawn */ }
